label: ai
topic: The enforcement gap — NDCs are not legally binding
text:
The Paris Agreement's enforcement architecture rests on what its architects called a facilitative compliance mechanism: countries that miss their pledges face no financial penalties, no trade sanctions, and no formal legal liability, but they must submit transparent reports on their emissions and their policy progress, creating a public record against which civil society, opposition parties, and future governments can hold them to account. Whether this is sufficient has been the subject of sustained debate since the agreement entered into force in November 2016.

Supporters of the voluntary approach point to the Kyoto Protocol as evidence that binding targets without domestic political buy-in achieve little. Canada withdrew from Kyoto in 2011 rather than face penalties for missing its targets, and Japan and Russia met their obligations largely through the collapse of Soviet-era industry rather than deliberate policy. The bottom-up, nationally determined approach, supporters argue, trades enforceability for universality: one hundred and ninety-five parties joined Paris where Kyoto covered fewer than forty. By making participation easy and targets self-determined, the agreement maximised coverage and created a framework that could be tightened over time through the ratchet mechanism.

Critics counter that the voluntary architecture simply externalises the accountability problem rather than solving it. When compliance depends on domestic politics, the agreement is only as durable as the governments that sign it. This vulnerability became apparent when the United States under President Trump announced its withdrawal in June 2017, barely eighteen months after the agreement was adopted. While the withdrawal was eventually reversed by the Biden administration, the episode demonstrated that the world's second-largest emitter could walk away without facing any formal consequences beyond diplomatic embarrassment.

The transparency framework is the agreement's principal safeguard against free-riding. Under the enhanced transparency framework finalised at COP24 in Katowice, all parties must submit biennial transparency reports containing detailed greenhouse gas inventories, information on progress toward their nationally determined contributions, and information on climate finance provided or received. These reports undergo technical expert review and a facilitative multilateral consideration of progress. The process is designed to be non-punitive but public, relying on the assumption that governments will not wish to be seen failing on the international stage.

Whether reputational pressure alone can drive the scale of transformation required remains an open question. The evidence from the first stocktake cycle, completed in 2023, suggests that while many countries have adopted new policies since Paris, the aggregate effect falls well short of the emission reductions needed to stay within the temperature targets. The enforcement gap, in short, is not a flaw in the agreement's design so much as a reflection of the limits of what sovereign states are willing to accept in the absence of a global authority with coercive power.
